perturbation.original_text: For a period commencing on the date of this Agreement and ending one (1) year thereafter (the “ Specified Period ”), each Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment any “Qualifying Person,” provided, however, that this section will not prevent either Party from: (a) engaging in discussions with a Qualifying Person where s/he has contacted such Party in response to (i) any general advertisement, job posting or similar notice; or (ii) an unsolicited resume or request for information from a Qualifying Person; or (b) engaging any recruiting firm or similar organization to identify or solicit persons for employment on behalf of such Party, or soliciting the employment of any specified officer or employee of a Party who is identified by any such recruiting firm or organization, in each case as long as such recruiting firm or organization does not directly target any officers or employees of a Party “ Qualifying Person ” shall mean any person who is an officer or employee of the other Party, who was introduced in person, by phone or email to the Party or its affiliates during the Specified Period in connection with evaluating a potential Transaction.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period commencing on the date of this Agreement and ending six (6) months thereafter (the “ Specified Period ”), each Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment any “Qualifying Person,” provided, however, that this section will not prevent either Party from: (a) engaging in discussions with a Qualifying Person where s/he has contacted such Party in response to (i) any general advertisement, job posting or similar notice; or (ii) an unsolicited resume or request for information from a Qualifying Person; or (b) engaging any recruiting firm or similar organization to identify or solicit persons for employment on behalf of such Party, or soliciting the employment of any specified officer or employee of a Party who is identified by any such recruiting firm or organization, in each case as long as such recruiting firm or organization does not directly target any officers or employees of a Party “ Qualifying Person ” shall mean any person who is an officer or employee of the other Party, who was introduced in person, by phone or email to the Party or its affiliates during the Specified Period in connection with evaluating a potential Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the non-solicitation period to six months may be unreasonably short and could be viewed as unenforceable depending on the specific circumstances and jurisdiction. California law views non-solicitation agreements with scrutiny.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements and any restraints on engaging in a profession, trade, or business. Although this is a non-solicitation clause, California courts carefully examine such clauses, and a six-month period may be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially given the context of a potential acquisition where relationships and knowledge transfer are significant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from a one-year non-solicitation period to six months creates a potential legal contradiction under California law, which views non-compete agreements with scrutiny. The provided snippet from California Code, BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While the contract text involves a non-solicitation agreement and not a non-compete, the Law Explanation points out that California courts carefully examine such clauses. A six-month period may be deemed an unreasonable restraint on trade, especially in the context of a potential acquisition. Thus, the shortened period introduces a significant risk of non-compliance and is a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Without limiting other remedies that may be available to the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party agrees that damages may not be an adequate remedy for any breach (whether actual or threatened) of the provisions of this Agreement and that accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek the remedies of injunction, specific performance or other equitable relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Without limiting other remedies that may be available to the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party agrees that damages will be the sole and exclusive remedy for any breach (whether actual or threatened) of the provisions of this Agreement and that accordingly, the Disclosing Party shall not be entitled to seek the remedies of injunction, specific performance or other equitable relief.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting remedies solely to damages and preventing injunctive relief may be unenforceable if it prevents the Disclosing Party from adequately protecting its trade secrets, particularly under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) provides for injunctive relief to prevent the dissemination of trade secrets. By limiting the remedies to only damages, the modified clause potentially prevents the Disclosing Party from obtaining an injunction, which is a key provision of the DTSA designed to stop the immediate and ongoing misappropriation of trade secrets, thereby contradicting the protections afforded by the DTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the intent of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) as it limits remedies to damages, preventing the Disclosing Party from seeking injunctive relief. The Law Explanation highlights that the DTSA provides for injunctive relief to prevent the dissemination of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, confirms that an owner of a trade secret may bring a civil action and that injunctive relief is an available remedy. The changed text eliminates this right, creating a direct legal contradiction because it prevents the Disclosing Party from utilizing a key provision of the DTSA designed to stop the misappropriation of trade secrets. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not usable, Snippet 1 confirms the availability of injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: Term Except as expressly set forth in Sections 4 and 6 herein, the confidentiality obligations in this Agreement will terminate on the second anniversary of the date of this Agreement; provided that (i) such termination shall in no way affect a breach of the terms of this Agreement which occurred prior to the date of such termination and (ii) the confidentiality obligations with respect to trade secrets included or reflected in the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall survive termination in perpetuity (unless the exception set forth in subsection (i) of Section 3 applies).
perturbation.changed_text: Term Except as expressly set forth in Sections 4 and 6 herein, the confidentiality obligations in this Agreement will terminate on the second anniversary of the date of this Agreement; provided that (i) such termination shall in no way affect a breach of the terms of this Agreement which occurred prior to the date of such termination and (ii) the confidentiality obligations with respect to trade secrets included or reflected in the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall terminate after five (5) years.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the protection of trade secrets to five years contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. This contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which many states have adopted.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which is adopted by most U.S. states, protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value. Setting a fixed termination date of five years contradicts the UTSA's provision for ongoing protection as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret. The modified clause removes the perpetual protection for trade secrets, making it inconsistent with the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modification limits the protection of trade secrets to a fixed term of five years, which contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain confidential and provide economic value, which aligns with the Perturbation Explanation. The changed text directly contradicts this by imposing a five-year limit, violating the standard for ongoing protection. The scraped snippets, while limited, generally support the existence of the Uniform Law Commission which promotes such acts.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of New York, USA, without regard to its conflicts of law provisions, and the Parties irrevocably submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the borough of Manhattan, New York, State of New York, USA, in respect of any claim, dispute or difference arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Nevada, USA, without regard to its conflicts of law provisions, and the Parties irrevocably submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Clark County, Nevada, State of Nevada, USA, in respect of any claim, dispute or difference arising out of or in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada, might create issues when the parties are located in different states (Netherlands and California). Nevada's laws on non-competes and trade secrets differ from New York's. This choice of law might contravene the principle that parties to a contract can agree on the governing law as long as there is a reasonable relationship to the jurisdiction and the choice does not violate a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law in a contract, Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws states that the choice of law will not be followed if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. If the NDA's provisions (e.g., regarding non-solicitation or trade secrets) are more permissive under Nevada law than under the law of California (where Qualcomm is located), and California has a materially greater interest in the dispute (e.g., because the harm occurred there), then applying Nevada law might violate California's fundamental policy and be deemed unenforceable by a court in California. The Restatement of Conflict of Laws is a well-regarded source of general law, even though it's not strictly a 'law' in and of itself.

perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from New York to Nevada introduces a potential contradiction. While parties can generally choose the governing law, this choice is not absolute. The Law Explanation, referencing Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws, states that a chosen law will not be followed if it violates a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. In this case, if Nevada law is more permissive regarding non-competes or trade secrets than California law (where Qualcomm is located), and California has a greater interest in the dispute, then applying Nevada law could violate California's policy. Since the original and changed text alters a fundamental aspect of the contract (governing law and jurisdiction) this change potentially violates legal principles related to choice of law, creating a contradiction.
